patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old elementary school teacher. You seem anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I keep getting these big bruises and my nose won’t stop bleeding sometimes.” Volunteer: large bruises after minor bumps (legs/arms), nosebleeds that take a long time to stop, gums bleed when brushing, and feeling wiped out after bleeding.

If asked about other bleeding: say cuts/scrapes ooze a long time, you bled more than expected after a tooth pull, periods are very heavy/longer lately, occasional blood in urine after hard workouts, and headaches after bad nosebleeds “like I’m drained.”

Do not mention unless specifically asked: you’ve had this since childhood, swelling/tightness over deep bruises, bleeding under bandages after shots/blood draws, and family members with “bleeding problems” or heavy dental bleeding.

Resist bringing up spotting after sex and fear of being suspected of abuse; only disclose if asked gently and reassured. Express misconceptions: ibuprofen might be causing it, maybe anemia/vitamin issue, gums bleed because you don’t floss. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, admit you avoid social plans and worry about costs, insurance coverage, and missing work.

Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
confounders: Heavy menstrual bleeding causing anemia and symptoms that can mimic other bleeding disorders
Recent low dietary vitamin K intake (restrictive diet) which can also prolong PT/INR
Intermittent use of NSAIDs for dysmenorrhea increasing mucosal bleeding risk without altering PT/aPTT

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency
required_evidence: Bleeding tendency (e.g., easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, post-procedural bleeding) with no thrombocytopenia
Prolonged PT/INR out of proportion to other findings; aPTT may be normal or mildly prolonged
Normal thrombin time (TT)
CBC platelets within reference range (rules out thrombocytopenic bleeding)
Clinical history consistent with congenital disorder (lifelong bleeding, family history) and absence of anticoagulant exposure or liver disease features

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be normal or decreased if recent/ongoing bleeding (anemia possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: May be normal or decreased if recent/ongoing bleeding

medications: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 25-50 IU/kg IV once for significant bleeding or prior to urgent invasive procedure; repeat dosing guided by clinical response and PT/INR
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) 10-20 mL/kg IV as alternative if PCC unavailable or contraindicated
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO/IV every 8-12 hours as adjunct for mucosal bleeding if no contraindication (e.g., active hematuria with clots, high thrombotic risk)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that increase bleeding risk (e.g., aspirin/NSAIDs) unless specifically indicated
Local hemostatic measures for minor bleeding (pressure, topical agents as available)
Plan peri-procedural hemostasis in advance with hematology; document diagnosis for future care
Genetic/family counseling and screening discussion when appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (earlier within 24-72 hours after acute bleed/procedure) with repeat coagulation testing and individualized bleeding management plan

must_include: Condition is an inherited deficiency/dysfunction of prothrombin (factor II) affecting blood clotting
Why PT/INR is prolonged and platelets are typically normal
Bleeding risk varies by severity; need to alert clinicians before procedures
For significant bleeding/procedures, factor replacement is needed (PCC preferred; FFP alternative)
Adjunct options for certain bleeding types (e.g., tranexamic acid for mucosal bleeding) and when it should be avoided
Monitoring plan (symptoms, repeat PT/INR; watch for thrombosis signs after PCC)
Discuss potential risks of therapy (thrombosis risk with PCC; volume overload/allergic reactions with FFP)
Review penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented; clarify that proposed hemostatic products are not penicillin
When to seek urgent care (uncontrolled bleeding, head injury, black stools, severe headache, shortness of breath/leg swelling)
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless instructed; check with clinicians before new medications/supplements
Carry medical alert information and inform dentists/surgeons in advance
Family implications and option for genetic counseling/testing

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history, severity, triggers (procedures/trauma), current meds/supplements, pregnancy status if relevant, and personal/family history of bleeding disorders; identify red flags needing urgent escalation.

Confirm/characterize coagulopathy and establish baseline: PT/INR, aPTT, CBC/platelets, fibrinogen; consider mixing study and factor II activity/antigen if available; assess for acquired causes (liver disease/vitamin K deficiency) as appropriate.

Interpret results consistent with congenital prothrombin deficiency (typically prolonged PT/INR with normal platelets; aPTT may be normal or mildly prolonged depending on level) and exclude alternative explanations.
State diagnosis and severity framing; document bleeding risk stratification and need for hemostatic planning before procedures.

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment options (PCC vs FFP, adjuncts like tranexamic acid with contraindications), monitoring plan, and lifestyle/precautions including family implications.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: verify and document penicillin allergy details and ensure proposed hemostatic agents are not penicillin; assess prior reactions to blood products if any.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., anticoagulants/antiplatelets, NSAIDs, herbal agents increasing bleeding) and with adjuncts such as tranexamic acid.

If clinically indicated (significant bleeding or planned procedure), prescribe factor replacement (4-factor PCC preferred; FFP alternative) and/or adjunct therapy such as tranexamic acid when appropriate; include dosing/administration plan per protocol and monitoring for thrombosis/volume overload.
Arrange hematology follow-up, peri-procedural planning, genetic counseling/testing discussion, and plan for repeat PT/INR and symptom monitoring.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net instructions, and clear next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent explains congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency as an inherited deficiency/dysfunction affecting clot formation and bleeding risk.
Agent correctly links prolonged PT/INR to reduced factor II activity in the common pathway and notes platelets are typically normal (distinguishing from platelet disorders).

Agent recommends factor replacement for significant bleeding or procedures, stating PCC is preferred and FFP is an alternative when PCC is unavailable or inappropriate.

Agent mentions adjunct therapy (e.g., tranexamic acid for mucosal bleeding such as epistaxis/menorrhagia/dental bleeding) and states when to avoid it (e.g., active intravascular clotting, history/high risk of thrombosis, gross hematuria/upper urinary tract bleeding due to clot retention risk, concomitant prothrombotic situations as clinically appropriate).

Agent avoids inappropriate recommendations (e.g., routine prophylactic anticoagulation, vitamin K as treatment for congenital deficiency unless evaluating for acquired causes, or platelet transfusion for isolated factor deficiency).

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate suspected/known congenital prothrombin deficiency with active significant bleeding or upcoming procedure; confirm labs; provide hemostatic plan with PCC/FFP and monitoring; complete safety checks before prescribing.
must_collect: current bleeding symptoms (site, onset, severity, duration)

steps: ASK: bleeding/procedure history, meds, allergies, thrombosis risk, red flags
ORDER_LAB: PT/INR, aPTT, CBC/platelets, fibrinogen; consider mixing study and factor II activity
GET_RESULTS: interpret prolonged PT/INR with normal platelets; assess aPTT; rule out acquired causes
DIAGNOSE: congenital prothrombin deficiency (severity-based bleeding risk)
EDUCATE: diagnosis explanation, procedure precautions, treatment options, monitoring, risks, lifestyle, genetic counseling

CHECK_INTERACTION: review meds/supplements; assess compatibility with tranexamic acid if used
PRESCRIBE: PCC for significant bleeding/procedure (or FFP alternative) ± tranexamic acid when appropriate; include monitoring for thrombosis/volume overload and repeat PT/INR
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: hematology coordination and peri-procedural plan

acceptable_alternatives: Curry-Jones syndrome (mosaic SMO-related craniosynostosis-polysyndactyly-anal anomalies syndrome)

non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric/colorectal surgery (or adult colorectal surgery as age-appropriate) for evaluation and definitive management of anal stenosis (e.g., anal dilation program vs anoplasty), including assessment for associated anorectal malformations

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Surgical evaluation within 1-2 weeks (same day/urgent if obstructive symptoms); reassess bowel symptoms and growth/nutrition within 2-4 weeks; genetics follow-up within 1-3 months

I can’t sit still during an attack—I pace and rock because it’s unbearable
These headaches have been happening in a cluster for the last 2 weeks, mostly at night
if_asked: The attacks often wake me from sleep about 1–2 hours after I fall asleep

Light and sound don’t really bother me much compared to how intense the pain is
I sometimes feel a little nauseated, but I’m not usually throwing up
Alcohol can trigger an attack during this period—even one drink
My dad had “cluster headaches” in his 30s and used to pace the house at night
Between attacks I feel mostly normal, just worried and tired
Separate from these, I also get brief electric-shock face pains on the same side when I brush my teeth or touch my cheek (I was told it’s trigeminal neuralgia)
hidden: Right eyelid droops a bit and the pupil looks smaller during an attack, but I thought it was just from crying
I get a sense of restlessness and agitation minutes before the pain hits, like I can’t settle
During this 2-week spell, I’ve been avoiding naps because falling asleep seems to bring one on
The headaches tend to come at nearly the same times each day, like a schedule
This same pattern happened last spring for about a month and then disappeared completely

I sometimes smoke and I’ve been smoking more lately because I’m stressed, and I don’t really want to talk about that
I tried a friend’s leftover pain pills once because I was desperate, and I’m worried you’ll judge me
misleading: It feels like it’s behind my eye and cheek, so I was sure it was a sinus infection

I searched online and got scared it might be a brain aneurysm because it’s so intense

general_concerns: Because his father had the same attacks, he believes the condition is purely genetic and that treatment won’t change the course—he just has to “ride it out.”
He thinks the attacks are caused by “sinus infections” or “allergies” because of the one-sided eye watering and nasal congestion, and he expects antibiotics or decongestants to fix it.
He believes oxygen therapy is dangerous and “can blow up” or cause dependence, so he avoids it even when offered.
He assumes triptans are basically the same as opioids and worries they will make him addicted or “mess up his heart,” so he underreports use and avoids discussing them.

If asked directly, disclose: attacks wake you 1–2 hours after sleep; 2–4/day; minimal light/sound sensitivity; mild nausea; alcohol triggers; dad had “cluster headaches”; normal between attacks; separate brief electric-shock face pain with touch/teeth brushing (trigeminal neuralgia).

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial), episodic
acceptable_alternatives: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia consistent with cluster headache
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency 1 every other day to 8 per day during a cluster period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation during attacks
Clustering pattern over weeks to months with remission (episodic) OR absence of remission >3 months (chronic)
No better explanation by secondary headache disorder; assess for red flags (sudden thunderclap onset, focal neuro deficits, fever/meningismus, new onset after age 50, immunosuppression, cancer)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (needed if considering NSAIDs, lithium, or contrast imaging)
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Typically normal (baseline if considering verapamil or other hepatically metabolized meds)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal (helps exclude giant cell arteritis when clinically relevant)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes at onset of attack
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous once at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as tolerated to effective dose (commonly 240-480 mg/day; sometimes higher) with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge while verapamil is titrated)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during active cluster period (common trigger)
Smoking cessation counseling (risk factor; may worsen course)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Headache diary to track timing, triggers, response to therapy
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist; consider MRI brain with pituitary views if atypical features/new onset/abnormal neuro exam
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess attack control and verapamil titration/ECG; sooner (24-72 hours) if uncontrolled attacks or adverse effects

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with severe one-sided attacks and autonomic symptoms
Attacks come in clusters over weeks/months; familial tendency can occur
Red flags that should prompt urgent evaluation (thunderclap onset, neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, new/worsening pattern, vision loss)
Two-part plan: abortive therapy for attacks (high-flow oxygen, triptan) and preventive therapy (verapamil) to reduce frequency
How to use oxygen and when to take sumatriptan (at onset), including daily maximum dose
Need for ECG monitoring and dose titration with verapamil; watch for bradycardia, dizziness, constipation
Prednisone is short-term bridge; discuss taper and side effects (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia, GI upset)
Screen for cardiovascular disease and contraindications to triptans (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe peripheral vascular disease)
Discuss potential medication adverse effects and when to seek care (chest pain after triptan, syncope, severe rash, shortness of breath)
Discuss pregnancy status if relevant and medication implications
Avoid alcohol during cluster period; identify personal triggers
Sleep regularity and stress management
Encourage headache diary and adherence to follow-up for titration/monitoring
Avoid medication overuse (do not exceed triptan limits; avoid frequent analgesic use)

Order baseline and follow-up ECG monitoring for verapamil initiation/titration; consider additional workup only if red flags/atypical features are present.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (abortive + preventive + bridge), safety warnings, and lifestyle/trigger counseling.
Arrange close follow-up for verapamil titration, ECG review, headache diary review, and reassessment of response/adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neuroendocrine tumor (NET) of the anal canal
required_evidence: Histopathologic confirmation from anal canal lesion biopsy (neuroendocrine morphology)
Immunohistochemistry positive for neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin and/or chromogranin A; INSM1 acceptable)
Tumor grade documentation (Ki-67 index and/or mitotic rate) to distinguish well-differentiated NET vs high-grade NEC
Staging evaluation with cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or MRI pelvis) and assessment for nodal/distant metastases
Consider functional imaging for staging (68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT for well-differentiated NET; FDG-PET/CT for high-grade NEC)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low or low-normal consistent with chronic blood loss/iron deficiency anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to high (reactive thrombocytosis can occur with iron deficiency/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated if inflammation/infection coexists)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low (iron deficiency); may be normal/high if significant inflammation present

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/ODT every 8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to colorectal surgery and medical oncology for definitive management based on grade and stage
Complete staging: MRI pelvis for local extent; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for metastases; consider 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (well-differentiated) or FDG-PET/CT (high-grade)

If localized well-differentiated NET: discuss local excision vs more extensive resection depending on size, invasion depth, margins, and nodal risk; consider somatostatin analog therapy if metastatic or symptomatic functional disease
If high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma: initiate platinum-based systemic therapy after staging (e.g., cisplatin or carboplatin plus etoposide) and coordinate with radiation oncology as indicated
Manage anemia: dietary iron counseling, assess ongoing bleeding, and consider IV iron if oral iron intolerant/ineffective
Screen for and address red flags requiring urgent evaluation (worsening bleeding, obstruction, severe pain, syncope)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for pathology review and staging plan; sooner (48-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia or ongoing significant bleeding

must_include: Diagnosis requires biopsy confirmation; explain what a neuroendocrine tumor is and that grade (Ki-67) drives treatment
Clarify why inflammatory bowel disease and infections (e.g., amoebiasis) are considered but cannot be confirmed without targeted stool/serologic testing and endoscopic/biopsy findings
Explain the need for staging imaging to determine if disease is localized or has spread
Treatment is individualized by stage and grade (local surgery for localized low-grade; systemic therapy for high-grade or metastatic disease)
Discuss supportive care for anemia (iron replacement, monitoring response, when IV iron/transfusion may be needed)
Set expectations for referrals (colorectal surgery/oncology) and timelines
Review warning symptoms that should prompt urgent care (heavy rectal bleeding, dizziness/fainting, severe pain, inability to pass stool/gas, fever)
Discuss potential side effects of iron (GI upset/constipation, dark stools) and how to take it (separate from calcium/antacids; consider vitamin C)
Acknowledge uncertainty until pathology/staging complete and invite questions
High-iron diet guidance and hydration/fiber to reduce constipation risk with iron therapy
Avoid NSAIDs if rectal bleeding is present unless clinician-directed
Encourage adherence to follow-up and completion of imaging/biopsy appointments

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and focus on: “He changed overnight—now weird rituals, nonstop hand-washing.” Volunteer that this started about 1 week ago, he checks things repeatedly, panics if stopped, has new tantrums/irritability, and can’t sleep because he gets out of bed to “make sure” things are right.

Do not volunteer hidden details unless directly probed: avoiding “wrong” words/numbers, slow eating/redoing bites, sudden messy handwriting, worry-related morning stomachaches, noise sensitivity.

Be reluctant to share resistant items unless the clinician is gentle: constant reassurance-seeking, scary “bad thoughts,” new bedwetting, one head-hitting episode.

medications: methylphenidate ER 18 mg PO each morning (school days)
melatonin 1 mg PO at bedtime as needed
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (can mimic/worsen neuropsychiatric symptoms)
Family history of OCD (increases baseline risk independent of streptococcal infection)
Stimulant side effects (methylphenidate can worsen anxiety, irritability, tics, and insomnia)
Negative rapid strep test despite positive throat culture (may delay recognition of GAS exposure)
School stress and sleep deprivation over the past month (can exacerbate tics/compulsions)

correct_diagnosis.primary: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections)
acceptable_alternatives: Tourette syndrome/tic disorder with comorbid OCD
Acute streptococcal pharyngitis with coincident neuropsychiatric symptoms
required_evidence: Abrupt (often overnight) onset or dramatic exacerbation of OCD and/or tics in a prepubertal child
Temporal association with Group A Streptococcus infection documented by positive throat testing (rapid antigen or culture) and/or rising streptococcal titers (ASO and/or anti-DNase B)
Episodic course with symptom exacerbations
Neurologic abnormalities during exacerbation (e.g., motor hyperactivity, choreiform movements, tics)
Exclusion of alternative neurologic/psychiatric diagnoses and medical mimics

expected_lab_results.Rapid strep A antigen (throat swab): May be positive if active GAS pharyngitis; can be negative if infection is remote
expected_lab_results.Throat culture (Group A Streptococcus): May be positive if active GAS carriage/infection; if negative does not exclude prior GAS-trigger
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: Often elevated and/or rising on repeat testing 2–4 weeks apart, consistent with recent GAS exposure
expected_lab_results.Anti-DNase B: Often elevated and/or rising on repeat testing, supportive of recent GAS exposure (may be more sensitive than ASO for skin/remote infection)
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Usually normal or mildly elevated; marked elevation suggests alternative/invasive infection
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - WBC: Often normal; may show mild leukocytosis if concurrent acute infection

medications: Azithromycin 12 mg/kg PO once daily on day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg PO once daily on days 2-5 (max 250 mg) if GAS pharyngitis confirmed or strongly suspected and penicillin allergy is urticarial
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max per label/weight-based limits)
non_pharmacological: Confirm GAS infection status (rapid test and/or throat culture); if titers used, repeat ASO/anti-DNase B in 2–4 weeks to assess for rising trend
Initiate evidence-based psychiatric care for OCD/tics: CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP); consider child psychiatry referral
School accommodations during exacerbation (reduced workload, breaks, quiet testing area)
Family education on episodic course, symptom tracking, and triggers; sleep hygiene and stress reduction

Consider neurology/child psychiatry evaluation to assess severity and exclude alternative diagnoses; reserve immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., steroids/IVIG) for specialist-directed care in severe, impairing cases after evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks to review throat culture/titers and symptom trajectory; earlier (48-72 hours) if worsening, dehydration, severe behavioral dysregulation, suicidality, or new neurologic deficits

must_include: PANDAS is a clinical diagnosis characterized by sudden onset/exacerbation of OCD/tics temporally linked to streptococcal infection
A positive throat test supports an active infection; elevated ASO/anti-DNase B support recent exposure but do not prove active infection
Other conditions can mimic PANDAS; evaluation aims to rule out alternatives and assess severity
Treat confirmed/suspected active strep infection with an appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic due to history of urticaria with amoxicillin
Neuropsychiatric symptoms often require CBT-ERP and sometimes medication management by specialists; antibiotics alone may not resolve OCD/tics
Expected timeline for infection symptom improvement (days) vs neuropsychiatric symptom course (often weeks with fluctuations)
Review red flags requiring urgent care: suicidal thoughts, severe aggression, inability to eat/drink, new focal neurologic deficits, chorea-like movements worsening, or high fever/neck stiffness
Discuss medication risks: macrolide GI upset, rare QT prolongation risk; advise to report palpitations/syncope
Set expectations about uncertainty and need for follow-up testing and multidisciplinary care
Sleep hygiene, regular routines, stress reduction, and symptom journaling (tics/OCD severity, infections, exposures)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief (seconds to 2 minutes) episodes of severe, electric shock-like facial pain in the distribution of one or more divisions of CN V
Pain is unilateral and follows trigeminal nerve territory (commonly V2/V3)
Attacks can be triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold air)
No objective neurologic deficit on exam (if present, consider secondary trigeminal neuralgia)
Between attacks, patient is typically pain-free or has minimal background pain

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal at baseline (used to monitor for carbamazepine-associated leukopenia/agranulocytosis)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal at baseline (monitor for thrombocytopenia)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Typically normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH risk with carbamazepine)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days as needed and tolerated (typical effective range 400-1200 mg/day in divided doses)
non_pharmacological: Identify and minimize triggers (chewing on affected side, cold wind, light facial touch) while maintaining oral hygiene

If atypical features, age <40, bilateral symptoms, sensory loss, or poor response: obtain MRI brain with and without contrast (plus MRA if available) to evaluate for secondary causes (e.g., MS, tumor, vascular compression)
If medication-refractory or intolerant: refer to neurology/neurosurgery to discuss procedures (microvascular decompression, percutaneous rhizotomy, stereotactic radiosurgery)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom response and adverse effects; repeat CBC and sodium within 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration and periodically thereafter

must_include: Condition involves irritation/misfiring of the trigeminal nerve causing brief, severe, shock-like facial pain
Triggers and pattern (brief attacks, unilateral distribution) fit trigeminal neuralgia
Red flags that would prompt urgent reassessment/imaging (new numbness/weakness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, vision changes, fever, rash, severe headache)
Carbamazepine is first-line and is taken daily to prevent attacks (not just during pain)
Titration plan and expectation that dose may be adjusted to balance pain control and side effects
Common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness) and safety advice (avoid driving until effects known)

Serious adverse effects requiring urgent care: rash/blistering (SJS/TEN), fever/sore throat or infection symptoms (agranulocytosis), unusual bruising/bleeding (thrombocytopenia), severe fatigue/confusion (possible hyponatremia)
Confirm allergies (penicillin causes hives; codeine causes severe nausea/vomiting) and clarify no prior reaction to anticonvulsants
Discuss pregnancy considerations/contraception if applicable (carbamazepine is teratogenic and can reduce hormonal contraceptive effectiveness)
Review key interaction risks (CYP induction: reduced efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, some antivirals/antifungals; additive sedation with alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids)
Avoid alcohol and other sedatives while starting/titrating due to additive CNS depression
Maintain good hydration and report symptoms of low sodium (headache, confusion, severe fatigue, seizures)
Keep a pain/trigger diary to guide titration and assess response

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key pain features to support trigeminal neuralgia vs mimics: unilateral distribution (V2/V3), shock-like paroxysms, duration seconds–2 min, triggerability (touch/chewing/brushing teeth), refractory period, and absence of neurologic deficits.

Screen for red flags and secondary causes requiring imaging/urgent evaluation: sensory loss, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, hearing/vision changes, severe headache, fever, rash/vesicles, jaw claudication, dental/sinus symptoms, cancer/immunosuppression.

Critical before prescribing: confirm reported allergies and clarify any prior reaction to anticonvulsants/aromatic AEDs; document penicillin hives and codeine severe nausea/vomiting; ensure no carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/lamotrigine severe rash history.

Critical before prescribing: review current meds/substances for carbamazepine interactions (CYP induction; reduced OCP/warfarin/antivirals/antifungals; additive sedation with alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids; macrolides/azoles can raise carbamazepine levels).

Assess pregnancy potential/contraception and relevant history: pregnancy status, plans, contraception type (hormonal efficacy reduced), breastfeeding; hepatic disease, blood dyscrasias, hyponatremia history, cardiac conduction disease.

Baseline monitoring prior to carbamazepine: CBC with differential/platelets, CMP including LFTs and sodium; consider pregnancy test if applicable. Establish baseline for rare hematologic toxicity, hepatotoxicity, and hyponatremia.
If new meds are revealed after initial interaction check, repeat/confirm interaction screening before finalizing prescription.
State working diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia when clinical pattern fits and no red flags are present; note plan for imaging/referral if red flags or atypical features.
Initiate first-line therapy carbamazepine with a titration approach and safety counseling; ensure allergy/contraindication/interaction checks completed before prescribing.

Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/titration, common and serious adverse effects, interaction risks, lifestyle advice (avoid alcohol/sedatives initially), hydration/low sodium symptoms, and pain/trigger diary.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and side effects, review lab results, and adjust dose; provide earlier return precautions for red flags or serious adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key pain features to support trigeminal neuralgia vs mimics: unilateral distribution (V2/V3), shock-like paroxysms, duration seconds–2 min, triggerability (touch/chewing/brushing teeth), refractory period, and absence of neurologic deficits.

Screen for red flags and secondary causes requiring imaging/urgent evaluation: sensory loss, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, hearing/vision changes, severe headache, fever, rash/vesicles, jaw claudication, dental/sinus symptoms, cancer/immunosuppression.

Critical before prescribing: confirm reported allergies and clarify any prior reaction to anticonvulsants/aromatic AEDs; document penicillin hives and codeine severe nausea/vomiting; ensure no carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/lamotrigine severe rash history.

Critical before prescribing: review current meds/substances for carbamazepine interactions (CYP induction; reduced OCP/warfarin/antivirals/antifungals; additive sedation with alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids; macrolides/azoles can raise carbamazepine levels).

Assess pregnancy potential/contraception and relevant history: pregnancy status, plans, contraception type (hormonal efficacy reduced), breastfeeding; hepatic disease, blood dyscrasias, hyponatremia history, cardiac conduction disease.

Baseline monitoring prior to carbamazepine: CBC with differential/platelets, CMP including LFTs and sodium; consider pregnancy test if applicable. Establish baseline for rare hematologic toxicity, hepatotoxicity, and hyponatremia.
If new meds are revealed after initial interaction check, repeat/confirm interaction screening before finalizing prescription.
State working diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia when clinical pattern fits and no red flags are present; note plan for imaging/referral if red flags or atypical features.
Initiate first-line therapy carbamazepine with a titration approach and safety counseling; ensure allergy/contraindication/interaction checks completed before prescribing.

Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/titration, common and serious adverse effects, interaction risks, lifestyle advice (avoid alcohol/sedatives initially), hydration/low sodium symptoms, and pain/trigger diary.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and side effects, review lab results, and adjust dose; provide earlier return precautions for red flags or serious adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent collects core trigeminal neuralgia symptom criteria (paroxysmal, brief, severe shock-like pain; unilateral trigeminal distribution; triggerable by innocuous stimuli; no persistent baseline pain) before diagnosing.

Agent explicitly screens for red flags that warrant urgent reassessment/imaging: new numbness/weakness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, vision changes, fever, rash, severe headache, or other atypical features.

Before prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check (including penicillin hives and codeine intolerance) and clarifies no prior severe cutaneous reaction to anticonvulsants/aromatic AEDs.

Before prescribing, agent performs and documents a contraindication check (pregnancy/teratogenicity considerations, hepatic disease, blood dyscrasias, hyponatremia risk, significant cardiac conduction disease) and addresses contraception if applicable.

Before prescribing, agent performs and documents a drug interaction check, including CYP induction effects (reduced efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, some antivirals/antifungals) and additive sedation with alcohol/benzodiazepines/opioids; agent advises avoidance of alcohol while starting/titrating.
Agent explains diagnosis in patient-friendly language, including trigeminal nerve misfiring/irritation causing brief severe shock-like facial pain and why the pattern fits trigeminal neuralgia.
Agent explains that carbamazepine is first-line, taken daily to prevent attacks (not PRN), and provides a titration plan with expectation of dose adjustment for efficacy/tolerability.
Agent counsels on common carbamazepine side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, unsteadiness) and provides safety advice (avoid driving/operating machinery until effects known).

Agent clearly lists serious adverse effects requiring urgent care: rash/blistering (SJS/TEN), fever/sore throat/infection symptoms (agranulocytosis), unusual bruising/bleeding (thrombocytopenia), severe fatigue/confusion (possible hyponatremia).

Agent provides lifestyle/self-management education: avoid sedatives/alcohol initially, maintain hydration, recognize low sodium symptoms (headache, confusion, severe fatigue, seizures), and keep a pain/trigger diary.
Agent orders appropriate baseline labs (CBC, LFTs, sodium; pregnancy test if applicable) and arranges timely follow-up to review results and adjust therapy.
Agent avoids unsafe actions: no prescribing carbamazepine without completing critical safety checks; no dismissal of red flags; no recommending opioids as primary therapy for trigeminal neuralgia.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 9-year-old girl in 4th grade. You are anxious and speak vaguely (“it hurts,” “it gets puffy sometimes”) unless asked specific questions. Start with: your knees and ankles swell and hurt, it keeps coming back, mornings are very stiff, you sometimes limp, and you get tired faster than friends. If asked about other joints or details, mention wrists/fingers hurting (hard to hold a pencil), one joint sometimes feels hot and looks bigger, worse after sitting still, occasional low fevers, sometimes morning back/hip stiffness, and pain can wake you at night when the knee is very swollen. Do not mention at first: avoiding sports/stairs, using OTC pain meds most days, missing school/gym, shoes tighter from ankle swelling. Only share these if asked about activities, school, or medicines. Be embarrassed about gym changing and hide swelling; minimize sadness/snappiness and admit it only if asked gently. Express misconceptions: “maybe growing pains,” “maybe from twisting my ankle,” “could it be Lyme?,” “maybe food like dairy.” If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, give clearer answers, and admit you sometimes pretend you’re fine so parents won’t worry. If costs come up, your parent worries about rationing steroids, thinks specialists/genetic tests are optional, and doubts PT is worth paying for.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. Speak clearly but with noticeable anxiety (worried, hurried). Start by saying: “My feet and ankles feel weak and I keep tripping, especially on stairs.” Early in the visit, volunteer: higher arches than before with unusual shoe wear, thinning lower legs, and fatigue on longer walks requiring careful stepping.

If asked about details, add: toes catch like foot doesn’t lift, tingling/numb patches, reduced pain/temperature awareness, new hand weakness (jars/buttons), frequent ankle sprains/rolling, and a family member with “weak ankles” and high arches.

Do NOT mention unless specifically prompted about falls/embarrassment, social avoidance, softer voice, or constipation; if asked directly, admit reluctantly.

Only reveal hidden issues (calluses/toe curling, balance worse in dark/uneven ground, shoe fit problems, hand cramps, unnoticed blisters) if asked about skin, footwear, balance, or injuries.

Express misconceptions: worry about vitamins/diabetes, pinched back nerve, “Parkinson’s getting worse” due to stress tremor, or poor circulation from calf cramps. Raise cost/insurance worries about genetic testing, braces/shoes, PT, and future insurability.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

medications: carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg 1 tablet three times daily
pramipexole 0.25 mg by mouth three times daily
gabapentin 300 mg by mouth at bedtime
ibuprofen 400 mg by mouth every 8 hours as needed for pain
comorbidities: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
Pes cavus with recurrent ankle sprains
confounders: Pramipexole-related orthostatic symptoms and somnolence may mimic autonomic dysfunction or fatigue
Recurrent ankle sprains and chronic foot pain can obscure progressive distal weakness and gait changes from neuropathy
Borderline-low vitamin B12 level can contribute to neuropathy symptoms despite normal MMA
Family history uncertainty/adoption limits pedigree assessment for hereditary neuropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) if genetic subtype specified
Distal hereditary motor neuropathy (if sensory findings are minimal/absent)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (especially peroneal/anterior tibialis) with foot drop
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Pes cavus/high arches and/or hammertoes; difficulty running; frequent ankle sprains
Family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities (often autosomal dominant, but can be sporadic)
No evidence of common acquired neuropathy causes on screening labs (normal/near-normal HbA1c/fasting glucose, TSH, B12 with normal methylmalonic acid, folate; ESR not elevated)
Electrodiagnostic testing consistent with hereditary neuropathy (demyelinating with slowed conduction velocities in CMT1 or axonal in CMT2) OR genetic confirmation

expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or not in diabetic range (does not explain neuropathy)
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Normal or not in diabetic range
expected_lab_results.Methylmalonic acid: Within reference range (no functional B12 deficiency)
expected_lab_results.CK (creatine kinase): Normal or mildly elevated (not suggestive of primary myopathy)

non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching (Achilles/hamstrings), balance and gait training; fall-prevention plan
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices if needed
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; custom orthotics; supportive footwear
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformity, recurrent ulcers, or significant gait impairment; consider surgical correction if indicated
Screen for and manage complications: ankle instability, falls, calluses/ulcers; regular foot care education
Avoid known neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine; caution with others depending on risk/benefit)
Assess for sleep, mood, and functional impact; offer supportive resources and patient advocacy groups

Open with: “I think people at school are watching me and talking about me, and it’s freaking me out.” Early in the interview, volunteer: feeling watched or sent messages on purpose, being on edge/scared in public, worse focus, poor sleep from racing thoughts, avoiding friends because it feels unsafe. patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but anxiously, worried something serious is wrong and about costs/missing work. Start by saying: you’ve felt wiped out and short of breath for ~2 weeks. Early in the visit, volunteer: racing heart on stairs, yellow eyes/skin, tea-colored morning urine, and that your parents are cousins and an older cousin had a “blood problem.” If asked, add: lightheaded on standing, headaches, paler skin, dull/full ache under left ribs, low-grade evening fevers, a bad cold a few weeks before this, and cold hands/feet. Do not offer unless specifically asked: craving/chewing ice, easier bruising, itchy eyes, worse fatigue after hot showers, mild unintentional weight loss. Only mention if the clinician asks gently about bowel habits/sex/alcohol: sometimes darker stools, low sex drive, and heavier weekend drinking to cope. Express misconceptions: fear of hepatitis/liver disease, dehydration, detox supplement side effect, or heart problem from chest tightness when anxious. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more willing to share sensitive details and discuss needed testing despite cost concerns.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA)

Macrocytosis may be present due to reticulocytosis (MCV normal-high)
Increased RDW consistent with mixed RBC populations/reticulocytosis
Note: confirmatory tests typically required but not provided here (direct antiglobulin test/Coombs, bilirubin, haptoglobin, peripheral smear)

Order confirmatory/etiologic workup if not already done: direct antiglobulin test (DAT), total/indirect bilirubin, haptoglobin, peripheral smear; evaluate secondary causes (e.g., lymphoproliferative disease, autoimmune disease, infections, medications)
Avoid potential hemolysis triggers/culprit drugs if suspected
Consider transfusion support if clinically indicated (symptomatic or severe anemia); coordinate with blood bank for crossmatch challenges in AIHA
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours for clinical response and repeat CBC/reticulocytes/LDH; then at least weekly until stable and tapering plan established

Why labs suggest hemolysis (low hemoglobin with high reticulocytes and high LDH)

Potential steroid adverse effects and red flags (infection, hyperglycemia, mood changes, GI upset) and when to seek care

Explain that transfusion can be more complex in AIHA but can be done safely when necessary
Review penicillin allergy history and ensure it is documented; reassure current plan does not require penicillin
Medication adherence and not stopping steroids abruptly
Infection precautions while on steroids and when to contact clinician
Follow-up plan and importance of repeat labs to ensure hemolysis is controlled

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 62-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor (high school education). You speak clearly but are anxious and worried about costs and missing shifts. Start by saying: “My belly keeps getting bigger and I feel really full after just a few bites.” Also volunteer: increasing fatigue with normal activity, ankle/lower-leg swelling by day’s end, jeans tighter despite not eating more, and dull pressure under the left ribs.

If asked, mention: shortness of breath when lying flat, harder-to-stop nosebleeds, easy bruising, one or two very dark/black stools, lightheadedness on standing, and vomiting after big meals (no visible blood).

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: spider-like red marks on chest/shoulders, gum bleeding, nighttime itching without rash, lower sex drive, and occasional “brain fog.”

Be resistant/embarrassed about alcohol: minimize intake; only admit morning vomiting after drinking if pressed. Also withhold blood in stool unless asked directly.

Offer misconceptions: think lip/eyelid swelling and belly pain attacks are “allergies/food reactions,” and yellow elbow bumps mean “high fat.” When clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more but keep asking about cheaper options and avoiding time off work.

correct_diagnosis.primary: GHRH-secreting tumor causing acromegaly (ectopic GHRH syndrome; GRFoma)
acceptable_alternatives: Acromegaly due to pituitary somatotroph adenoma (if ectopic source not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Elevated age-adjusted IGF-1
Failure of GH suppression on oral glucose tolerance test (GH nadir at 120 min not suppressed)
Elevated plasma GHRH supporting ectopic source (GRFoma)
Exclude other pituitary hormone deficiencies/excess (prolactin, TSH/free T4, AM cortisol) prior to definitive therapy

expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Elevated above age- and sex-adjusted reference range
expected_lab_results.Growth hormone (random): Elevated or inappropriately normal for clinical context (pulsatile secretion; not reliably low)
expected_lab_results.Oral glucose tolerance test (GH nadir at: Fails to suppress (e.g., nadir > 1 ng/mL; often > 0.4 ng/mL with ultrasensitive assays)
expected_lab_results.GHRH (plasma): Elevated (supports ectopic GHRH secretion/GRFoma rather than primary pituitary adenoma)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal or mildly elevated (possible stalk effect/pituitary hyperplasia); not markedly high
expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal (or inappropriately normal if central hypothyroidism present; evaluate with free T4)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Usually normal; assess for central hypothyroidism prior to surgery/medical therapy
expected_lab_results.AM cortisol (8 AM): Normal if intact HPA axis; low would indicate adrenal insufficiency needing steroid coverage

medications: Octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks (titrate to 30 mg IM every 4 weeks based on IGF-1/GH and symptoms)
Lanreotide depot 90 mg deep SC every 4 weeks (alternative to octreotide; titrate 60–120 mg every 4 weeks)
Pegvisomant 10 mg SC daily (add-on or alternative if IGF-1 remains elevated despite somatostatin analog; titrate by IGF-1 to 20–30 mg daily)
Cabergoline 0.5 mg PO twice weekly (consider add-on if mild biochemical activity and/or prolactin co-secretion; titrate as tolerated)
non_pharmacological: Localize source of ectopic GHRH secretion with cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI chest/abdomen/pelvis) and functional imaging if needed; refer to endocrinology and surgical oncology
Definitive management: surgical resection of the GHRH-secreting tumor when feasible

If unresectable/metastatic: long-term somatostatin analog therapy; consider tumor-directed therapy (e.g., debulking, liver-directed therapy, systemic therapy) per tumor type and multidisciplinary team
Assess and manage acromegaly complications: blood pressure, glucose/HbA1c, sleep apnea evaluation, cardiomyopathy/echo if indicated, colonoscopy per acromegaly guidance, arthropathy management
Pituitary MRI to assess for pituitary hyperplasia/adenoma and baseline anatomy (ectopic GHRH can cause pituitary hyperplasia)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck IGF-1 (and GH if used) 8–12 weeks after starting or changing medical therapy; then every 3–6 months until controlled, then every 6–12 months. Post-resection: repeat IGF-1 and OGTT GH suppression at ~12 weeks.

must_include: Labs show acromegaly physiology: high IGF-1 and GH not suppressing with glucose
High plasma GHRH suggests GH excess is driven by a GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) rather than only a pituitary problem
Need additional imaging to find the tumor source and guide definitive treatment
Differentials considered (Sandhoff disease, hereditary angioedema, familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency) are not supported by the GH/IGF-1/GHRH pattern
Definitive therapy is removal of the GHRH-secreting tumor when possible
Medical therapy (somatostatin analogs; pegvisomant; cabergoline in select cases) can control hormones and symptoms before/after surgery or if unresectable
Monitoring plan: IGF-1-guided dose adjustments and follow-up timing

Review medication adverse effects: somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes, bradycardia), pegvisomant (liver enzyme elevation), cabergoline (nausea, orthostasis; rare valvulopathy at higher cumulative doses)
Discuss warning symptoms and when to seek care (severe abdominal pain, jaundice, symptomatic hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia, severe headaches/visual changes)
Confirm allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify that proposed therapies are not penicillin-based; document reaction types
Importance of managing cardiovascular and metabolic risks (BP, glucose, lipids), sleep apnea evaluation, and routine cancer screening (especially colonoscopy) in acromegaly
Encourage adherence to follow-up labs and imaging to confirm biochemical control and tumor management
Discuss symptom tracking (headaches, sweating, joint pain, ring/shoe size changes) as part of monitoring response

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key acromegaly symptoms, timeline, comorbidities (HTN/DM/OSA), tumor-source clues (pulmonary/GI symptoms), meds, pregnancy status if relevant, and allergies (penicillin, shellfish) with reaction types.

Order/confirm diagnostic and baseline safety labs for suspected GRFoma-driven acromegaly: IGF-1, GH with oral glucose suppression test (OGTT), plasma GHRH, pituitary panel as needed, A1c/fasting glucose, CMP/LFTs, lipid panel; consider baseline ECG if bradycardia risk.

Interpret results to confirm acromegaly physiology (elevated IGF-1, GH not suppressing) and elevated GHRH supporting ectopic GHRH source (GRFoma). Review baseline LFTs/glucose prior to medical therapy.
Make diagnosis of acromegaly due to suspected GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) and document rationale; explicitly note why listed differentials are not supported by the hormone pattern.

Explain diagnosis and need for additional imaging to localize tumor source (e.g., chest/abdomen/pelvis imaging; pituitary MRI if not already done) and outline definitive vs medical management options.

Order localization and complication evaluation: imaging to find ectopic source (CT/MRI chest/abdomen/pelvis; consider somatostatin-receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT if available), plus screening for complications (sleep study referral, colonoscopy referral, echocardiogram if clinically indicated).
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm/document allergies and reaction types (penicillin, shellfish) and verify proposed therapies are not penicillin-based.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review interactions (e.g., somatostatin analog effects on glucose control; QT/bradycardia considerations; dopamine agonist interactions if cabergoline used).

Assess contraindications/precautions prior to prescribing: gallbladder disease history, uncontrolled diabetes, baseline bradycardia/arrhythmia, liver disease, pregnancy/lactation, valvular disease history if considering cabergoline.

Initiate appropriate medical therapy when indicated (e.g., long-acting somatostatin analog; consider pegvisomant or cabergoline in select cases) as bridge to definitive tumor resection or if unresectable; include monitoring plan.
Explain treatment, adverse effects, warning symptoms, and lifestyle/complication risk management; reinforce follow-up labs/imaging and symptom tracking.

Arrange follow-up timing for IGF-1-guided titration and safety monitoring (e.g., 4–12 weeks after therapy change depending on agent; LFT checks for pegvisomant; glucose monitoring; imaging follow-up).
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-net instructions, and documented plan.

patient.chief_complaint: I get tired and weak really fast, and my eyelids droop by the end of the day.

volunteer: My eyelids start to droop as the day goes on, especially if I’m reading or on my phone a lot.
I get weak really quickly when I climb stairs or carry groceries, but after I rest a bit I can do more.
By afternoon my speech gets kind of slurred or “mumbly,” like my mouth is tired.
Chewing gets tiring halfway through meals, especially meat or crusty bread.
if_asked: I sometimes choke or cough on drinks when I’m tired.
My head feels heavy and I have trouble keeping it up late in the day.
I’m much worse in the evening or after being sick; mornings are usually better.
I get short of breath when I’m exhausted or if I try to talk and walk at the same time.
I’ve had this kind of weakness since I was a kid, but it’s been more noticeable the last couple of years.
I’ve always been “clumsy” and avoided sports because my muscles give out fast.
hidden: I’ve always been smaller and skinnier than others and had a hard time gaining muscle no matter what I do.
I get very wiped out after even a mild cold, and it can take me weeks to feel normal again.
I’ve had episodes where my voice gets very quiet or nasal when I’m tired, and people ask if I’m sick.
I sometimes wake up with headaches and feel unrefreshed, like I didn’t breathe well overnight.
My stomach is often upset—loose stools or cramping on and off—so I just thought I had a “sensitive stomach.”
I bruise easily and my periods can be heavy (or I’ve been told my blood counts were “a bit off” before), but I never connected it to the weakness.
resistant: Sometimes I choke on food in public and it’s embarrassing, so I avoid eating with coworkers.
I’ve had a couple of times where I wet myself a little because I couldn’t get to the bathroom fast when I was exhausted, and I didn’t want to mention it.
I avoid intimacy sometimes because my arms and neck get tired and I feel self-conscious about it.
misleading: I’m pretty sure it’s just anxiety because when I’m stressed my breathing feels tight and my voice changes.
I wondered if it’s low iron or a thyroid problem because I’m always tired and feel cold easily.
I thought maybe it was multiple sclerosis because the weakness comes and goes and I get clumsy.
I’ve been blaming it on being out of shape since exercise makes me crash.
noise: I get tension headaches from staring at screens.
My seasonal allergies make my eyes itchy and watery.
I have occasional heartburn after spicy food.
I sometimes get mild knee pain after long walks.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking community college student/barista. Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, and my eyelids droop by the end of the day.” Volunteer early: eyelids droop with reading/phone use; you get weak on stairs/groceries but improve after rest; afternoon slurred/mumbly speech; chewing fatigue mid-meal (meat/crusty bread). If asked, add: occasional coughing/choking on drinks when tired; heavy head/neck late day; worse evenings/after illness, mornings better; short of breath when exhausted or talking while walking; lifelong clumsiness/avoided sports. Do NOT mention unless directly asked with reassurance: embarrassment about choking in public; occasional small urine leakage when exhausted; avoiding intimacy due to fatigue/self-consciousness. Only reveal hidden issues if asked broadly about other symptoms: small/thin, slow recovery after colds, nasal/quiet voice when tired, unrefreshed mornings/headaches, intermittent loose stools/cramps, easy bruising/heavy periods/“low counts.” Express misconceptions: blame anxiety, low iron/thyroid, MS, or being out of shape. Bring up cost worries and fear of debt/time off. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears, and share more details.

confounders: Fluctuating fatigable weakness with ptosis can mimic autoimmune myasthenia gravis, but AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies are negative

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (unspecified subtype) with suspected glycosylation pathway defect
Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to GMPPB mutation
Congenital disorder of glycosylation presenting with neuromuscular junction dysfunction (CMS phenotype)
required_evidence: Chronic fatigable weakness since infancy/childhood (ptosis/ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms, limb-girdle weakness) with fluctuation and worsening with exertion/illness
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction disorder (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No acetylcholine receptor or MuSK antibodies (to distinguish from autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Genetic confirmation of a CMS glycosylation-pathway gene (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1, ALG14/ALG2) OR strong phenotype consistent with glycosylation-defect CMS pending genetic testing
History of worsening weakness/respiratory symptoms with macrolides (e.g., azithromycin), supporting NMJ vulnerability

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal for CMS; no specific diagnostic abnormality expected
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal unless intercurrent infection/illness triggers symptom worsening
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal (monitor if using beta-agonists such as albuterol/salbutamol, which can lower potassium)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be elevated in chronic hypoventilation or low in acute respiratory distress depending on scenario
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal; used for baseline renal function before certain medications and overall assessment

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake (titrate to effect and tolerability; avoid excessive dosing if worsens symptoms)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or 4 mg PO twice daily) as a symptomatic adjunct in selected CMS subtypes; monitor heart rate and potassium
Ephedrine 25 mg PO three times daily as alternative adjunct if beta-agonist not tolerated/available; monitor blood pressure and heart rate
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides like azithromycin/erythromycin, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products, and certain anesthetic/neuromuscular blockers); provide a written avoidance list
Respiratory safety planning: assess for nocturnal hypoventilation and bulbar dysfunction; consider sleep study/pulmonary function testing (FVC, MIP/MEP) and noninvasive ventilation if indicated
Referral to neuromuscular specialist/genetics for confirmatory testing and subtype-specific therapy selection; consider CK and antibody testing if not already done
Physical/occupational therapy with energy conservation strategies; avoid overexertion that precipitates fatigue
Vaccination and prompt treatment of respiratory infections to reduce exacerbations; individualized plan for perioperative/anesthesia precautions
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 2-4 weeks after medication initiation or dose adjustment; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if any respiratory/bulbar worsening or significant adverse effects

must_include: This is a genetic (non-autoimmune) disorder of neuromuscular transmission; symptoms can fluctuate and worsen with illness/exertion
Glycosylation-pathway CMS affects how proteins at the neuromuscular junction function, leading to fatigable weakness
Genetic confirmation guides the best medication choice and helps predict which drugs may help or worsen symptoms
Purpose and expected benefits of pyridostigmine and that dosing is titrated to symptoms
Adjunct options (albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) may help in some CMS glycosylation subtypes; monitoring for palpitations, tremor, blood pressure changes, and potassium shifts
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, weak cough, trouble swallowing, choking, or inability to speak full sentences
Review and document allergies: azithromycin caused worsened weakness/shortness of breath; penicillin caused urticarial rash
Medication avoidance counseling: macrolides and other NMJ-impairing drugs can precipitate dangerous weakness; advise to alert all clinicians/pharmacists
Discuss that autoimmune immunosuppressive therapies used in myasthenia gravis are generally not indicated unless autoimmune MG is proven
Energy conservation, pacing, and avoiding overheating/overexertion that increases fatigability
Infection prevention and early evaluation for respiratory infections
Perioperative/anesthesia precautions and the need to inform surgeons/anesthesiologists about CMS and drug sensitivities

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (fatigable weakness pattern), severity, respiratory/bulbar red flags, triggers, functional impact, current meds, and prior genetic/neuromuscular workup relevant to congenital myasthenic syndromes (CMS).
Critical before prescribing: confirm and document allergies/adverse reactions, specifically azithromycin-associated worsened weakness/shortness of breath and penicillin urticarial rash.

Critical contraindication check before ordering: ensure ordered tests are appropriate and safe; order labs that support safe initiation/monitoring of therapy (e.g., electrolytes if considering beta-agonist/ephedrine) and any confirmatory/phenotyping tests if not already completed.
Review returned data (if available) to confirm no immediate safety issues (e.g., potassium abnormalities) and to support diagnosis/management planning.

Synthesize history and available data to identify CMS with glycosylation defect (genetic, non-autoimmune neuromuscular transmission disorder) and document rationale and differentials (autoimmune MG, motor neuron disease, myopathy) as appropriate.
Critical before prescribing: screen for drug-drug interactions with planned therapies (pyridostigmine; possible albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) and current medications.

Initiate/titrate first-line symptomatic therapy (typically pyridostigmine) and consider adjunct (albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) depending on subtype and response; ensure safety checks completed and provide monitoring parameters.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/risks, medication avoidance (macrolides/NMJ-impairing drugs), red flags, lifestyle pacing, infection prevention, and perioperative/anesthesia precautions.
Arrange timely follow-up to assess response, side effects, and need for dose adjustment/subtype-directed therapy; include contingency plan for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and ensuring urgent-care instructions are clear.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (fatigable weakness pattern), severity, respiratory/bulbar red flags, triggers, functional impact, current meds, and prior genetic/neuromuscular workup relevant to congenital myasthenic syndromes (CMS).
Critical before prescribing: confirm and document allergies/adverse reactions, specifically azithromycin-associated worsened weakness/shortness of breath and penicillin urticarial rash.

Critical contraindication check before ordering: ensure ordered tests are appropriate and safe; order labs that support safe initiation/monitoring of therapy (e.g., electrolytes if considering beta-agonist/ephedrine) and any confirmatory/phenotyping tests if not already completed.
Review returned data (if available) to confirm no immediate safety issues (e.g., potassium abnormalities) and to support diagnosis/management planning.

Synthesize history and available data to identify CMS with glycosylation defect (genetic, non-autoimmune neuromuscular transmission disorder) and document rationale and differentials (autoimmune MG, motor neuron disease, myopathy) as appropriate.
Critical before prescribing: screen for drug-drug interactions with planned therapies (pyridostigmine; possible albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) and current medications.

Initiate/titrate first-line symptomatic therapy (typically pyridostigmine) and consider adjunct (albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) depending on subtype and response; ensure safety checks completed and provide monitoring parameters.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/risks, medication avoidance (macrolides/NMJ-impairing drugs), red flags, lifestyle pacing, infection prevention, and perioperative/anesthesia precautions.
Arrange timely follow-up to assess response, side effects, and need for dose adjustment/subtype-directed therapy; include contingency plan for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and ensuring urgent-care instructions are clear.

nl_assertions: Agent asks specifically about fluctuating fatigable weakness, exertional worsening, diurnal variation, and triggers such as illness, overheating, and prolonged activity.
Agent screens for bulbar and respiratory red flags (dyspnea, weak cough, dysphagia, choking, inability to speak full sentences) and escalates urgency if present.
Agent distinguishes CMS from autoimmune myasthenia gravis by stating CMS is genetic/non-autoimmune and does not default to immunosuppression unless autoimmune MG is proven.

Agent explicitly explains that glycosylation-pathway CMS affects neuromuscular junction protein function, leading to fatigable weakness, and that genetic confirmation guides medication choice and predicts benefit/harm.
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents allergy check including azithromycin-related worsened weakness/shortness of breath and penicillin urticarial rash.
Before prescribing, agent performs a drug interaction check against the current medication list and planned therapies.

Before prescribing and before ordering, agent performs contraindication checks (e.g., cardiac disease/arrhythmia risk with beta-agonists/ephedrine; electrolyte abnormalities; pregnancy considerations if applicable).
Agent provides treatment counseling: pyridostigmine purpose, expected benefits, common side effects, and that dosing is titrated to symptoms and tolerability.
Agent discusses adjunct options (albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine) as subtype-dependent, including monitoring for palpitations, tremor, blood pressure changes, and potassium shifts.

Agent provides medication avoidance counseling: macrolides and other NMJ-impairing drugs can precipitate dangerous weakness; advises patient to alert clinicians/pharmacists and to carry an updated medication/allergy list.

Agent gives lifestyle education: pacing/energy conservation, avoiding overheating/overexertion, infection prevention and early evaluation for respiratory infections, and perioperative/anesthesia precautions including informing surgeons/anesthesiologists.
Agent’s plan includes follow-up with objective reassessment (functional measures, symptom diary, side effects) and clear return/ER precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Safe evaluation and initiation of symptomatic therapy for suspected/confirmed glycosylation-pathway CMS with complete safety checks and required counseling.
steps: ASK: characterize fluctuating weakness, triggers, functional impact; screen for bulbar/respiratory red flags; review meds and prior genetic confirmation
CHECK_ALLERGY: document azithromycin worsened weakness/shortness of breath; penicillin urticarial rash; clarify severity and timing
ORDER_LAB: order baseline electrolytes (including potassium) and other monitoring/phenotyping labs as indicated; perform contraindication_check before ordering
GET_RESULTS: review for safety (e.g., potassium) and readiness for adjunct therapy if considered
DIAGNOSE: CMS with glycosylation defect (or suspected pending genetics), note non-autoimmune nature and rationale
CHECK_INTERACTION: evaluate interactions with planned pyridostigmine and any adjunct options
PRESCRIBE: start/titrate pyridostigmine; consider adjunct albuterol/salbutamol or ephedrine only with contraindication awareness and monitoring plan
EDUCATE: deliver all communication milestones (diagnosis, treatment, concerns/allergies & avoidance, lifestyle/anesthesia precautions, red flags)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: early follow-up for titration and monitoring; provide urgent-care instructions

minimal_paths.description: If red-flag respiratory/bulbar symptoms are present, prioritize urgent evaluation while still completing critical safety documentation.
steps: ASK: immediately assess airway/breathing red flags and dysphagia/aspiration risk; determine need for ED/EMS
CHECK_ALLERGY: document key allergies/adverse reactions (azithromycin, penicillin) for safe acute care
EDUCATE: instruct urgent care/ED evaluation for worsening shortness of breath, weak cough, trouble swallowing, choking, inability to speak full sentences; advise avoiding NMJ-impairing drugs
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: arrange expedited neuromuscular follow-up after acute evaluation

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs DIAGNOSE after initial history and allergy check, then orders baseline labs for medication safety/monitoring before prescribing.

If genetic subtype is not yet confirmed, agent frames diagnosis as suspected CMS (glycosylation pathway) and focuses on symptomatic management and referral/testing while still providing full counseling and safety checks.
Adjunct therapy deferred: agent starts pyridostigmine only, provides monitoring and avoidance counseling, and plans reassessment before considering albuterol/ephedrine.

general_concerns: Thinks nephritis is basically the same as a simple urinary tract infection and should clear with a short course of antibiotics.
Believes that if her urine looks normal some days, her kidneys must be fine and follow-up tests aren’t necessary.
Assumes drinking large amounts of water and “kidney cleanse” teas can flush out inflammation and replace medical treatment.
Thinks swelling is only from eating salty foods and not related to kidney inflammation.
Believes over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen are safe to use regularly for flank discomfort because they’re non-prescription.
economic_concerns: Worries that repeated blood/urine tests are 'extra' and will lead to unnecessary charges.
Thinks the cheapest option is to wait and see rather than start medications or see a kidney specialist.
Concerned about taking unpaid time off work for appointments and lab visits.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My pee has looked kind of dark and I’m puffy around my eyes in the mornings.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tea/cola-colored urine for a few days, eyelid/facial puffiness in the morning, mild ankle swelling by day’s end, and more fatigue.

If asked, add: you’re peeing less despite similar fluids, urine sometimes looks foamy, dull low back/side ache, headaches for 2 days, mild shortness of breath on stairs, and a sore throat 2 weeks ago that resolved.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: 5-lb weight gain this week, tighter rings/deeper sock marks, decreased nighttime urination, and heavy ibuprofen use for a toothache this past week.

If asked about supplements/drugs, be reluctant and minimize; you used a friend’s “muscle-building” pills briefly and don’t want to discuss.

Express misconceptions: maybe dehydration/energy drinks, kidney stone, or UTI (one mild burn once). Voice cost/time worries about repeated labs, meds, or nephrology. If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and agree to needed testing if explained clearly.

medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent NSAID use (can worsen renal function and mimic/worsen nephritis)
Possible urinary tract inflammation (trace leukocyte esterase/WBCs without nitrites) complicating interpretation of hematuria
Recent upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (raises consideration of post-infectious glomerulonephritis vs lupus flare)
Menses within the last 48 hours (potential contamination contributing to hematuria on urinalysis)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute nephritic syndrome (suspected acute glomerulonephritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) pending medication/exposure history
Acute kidney injury (AKI) of unclear etiology pending urinalysis and imaging
required_evidence: Acute kidney injury pattern on labs (rising serum creatinine and BUN with reduced eGFR)
Clinical nephritic features (e.g., hematuria/cola-colored urine, edema, hypertension) OR documented active urinary sediment if available
No evidence of isolated pre-renal azotemia alone (requires context such as volume depletion; if present, must be addressed but does not exclude nephritic syndrome)
Electrolyte/acid-base assessment consistent with impaired renal function (possible hyperkalemia and/or low bicarbonate)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (dilutional hyponatremia possible with fluid retention)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (hyperkalemia possible with reduced GFR)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated if non-anion gap metabolic acidosis present
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal in acute nephritic presentations; may be low-normal if significant renal dysfunction

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate to urine output and volume status
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (if persistent hypertension despite diuresis or if diuresis not appropriate)
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO three times daily for up to 48 hours (if hyperkalemia present without ECG instability)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; restrict sodium (e.g., <2 g/day) and fluid if volume overloaded
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast when possible); review and stop potential offending agents
Monitor strict intake/output and daily weights
Renal diet considerations if significant AKI (potassium and phosphate restriction as appropriate)
Urgent nephrology consultation if rapidly rising creatinine, oliguria, severe hypertension, suspected RPGN, or refractory electrolyte/acid-base issues
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck BMP (including creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 24-48 hours (sooner if hyperkalemia, acidosis, oliguria, or clinical deterioration); outpatient follow-up within 1 week if stable and improving

must_include: Kidney inflammation is suspected based on reduced kidney function on labs
This can cause fluid retention, higher blood pressure, and electrolyte/acid-base changes
Further testing (e.g., urine studies and sometimes kidney imaging/serologies) is often needed to identify the cause and guide targeted therapy
Main goals: protect kidney function, control blood pressure/edema, and prevent complications like high potassium
Medication plan includes diuretics for fluid overload and blood pressure agents as needed
Avoiding nephrotoxic medications is essential
Red flags requiring urgent care: reduced urine output, worsening swelling/shortness of breath, chest pain, severe headache/vision changes, weakness/palpitations (possible hyperkalemia)
Discuss expected monitoring frequency and when to seek emergency evaluation
Low-sodium diet and fluid guidance tailored to swelling/blood pressure
Avoid NSAIDs and over-the-counter supplements that may harm kidneys
Adherence to follow-up labs and appointments to track kidney recovery

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit nephritis-relevant history: onset of edema/hematuria/foamy urine/oliguria, recent infection, autoimmune symptoms, medication/NSAID use, comorbid HTN/DM, pregnancy status if relevant, and baseline kidney disease.

Order initial evaluation to confirm kidney inflammation pattern and severity: BMP (Cr/BUN, K, HCO3), urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein quantification (UPCR/UACR), CBC; consider complements/ANA/ANCA/anti-GBM/hepatitis tests depending on presentation.
Review labs to identify AKI vs CKD, proteinuria/hematuria/casts, electrolyte/acid-base issues (esp. hyperkalemia), and anemia/inflammation.
State suspected nephritis (glomerular inflammation) based on reduced kidney function and urine findings; communicate need for further testing/possible imaging/serologies to determine cause.
Explain diagnosis, expected course, red flags, lifestyle changes (low sodium, fluid guidance), and avoidance of nephrotoxins (NSAIDs/supplements).
Critical safety step before prescribing any new medication.
Non-critical but expected check for interactions (e.g., ACEi/ARB with K-raising drugs; diuretics with lithium; NSAIDs with ACEi/diuretics).

Initiate supportive therapy if indicated by findings (e.g., loop diuretic for edema; antihypertensive such as ACEi/ARB if appropriate and potassium/renal function allow), and explicitly avoid nephrotoxic meds.
Arrange close monitoring of renal function, electrolytes, BP, weight, and urine protein; define timeframe and escalation/ED criteria.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.
